rxn_class_summary: Continuous-flow electrochemical 1,2-arylheteroarylation of alkenes using aryl bromides and cyanopyridines to give 1,2-diarylethane products
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of both the aryl bromide (to aryl radical via debromination) and the cyanopyridine (to a persistent radical anion); concurrent two-substrate cathodic SET enables radical-radical cross-coupling

key_intermediates.name: transient benzylic/alkyl carbon-centered radical from aryl radical addition to alkene (35)

electron_flow: At the cathode, the aryl bromide is reduced and loses bromide to give an aryl radical that adds across the alkene, generating a transient C-radical; in parallel the cyanopyridine is cathodically reduced to a persistent radical anion, and radical-radical cross-coupling between the transient and persistent radicals delivers the product. Et3N serves as the sacrificial reductant oxidized at the anode.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 4 fully suppressed with 3.0 equiv TEMPO; TEMPO-aryl adduct detected by HRMS, indicating aryl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropylvinyl)benzene (radical clock 31)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 32 isolated in 37% yield, supporting a carbon-centered radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: methyl 4-bromobenzoate (1)
probe_reagents.observation: CV shows irreversible reduction at -1.86 V (vs Ag/AgCl) assigned to reductive debromination, with a subsequent quasi-reversible wave (Epc = -2.27 V) for the methyl benzoate radical

probe_reagents.name: styrene (2)
probe_reagents.observation: No reduction wave from 0 to -2.5 V; electrochemically inert under conditions

probe_reagents.name: 4-cyanopyridine (3)
probe_reagents.observation: Quasi-reversible redox wave (Epc = -1.81 V, Epa = -1.61 V), characteristic of a persistent radical anion; similar potential to 1 enables concurrent cathodic reduction

notes: Aliphatic alkenes are incompatible under the standard conditions. Mechanism relies on the persistent radical effect: cyanopyridine radical anion accumulates as the persistent partner while the alkyl radical is transient, biasing selective cross-coupling over homocoupling. Batch electrolysis gave much lower yields than continuous flow under identical conditions.
